posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
• 2006 Domaine Denis Bachelet Gevrey-Chambertin Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin (2/18/2023)
    This was absolutely terrific last night, I mean I was blown away! It had an astounding vinous quality, that enhanced already gorgeous, dark red wild cherry fruit. The fruit is crunchy, and rich, also weightless, and so fine. The house seems to get the most perfect balance between fruit, mineral and earth/soil perceptions. What a terrific place right now I believe it will improve for quite a few years to come. If you’re sitting on this, or lucky enough…Evocelles, Corbeaux, or Charmes, give them years, and be happy when you reflect on your cellar knowing they are there. (95 points)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Very good to read this note. I have a range of 2006 Bachelet, but have only opened the Bourgogne so far and that has been…well, not very good. Or rather, one promising bottle, then two not even good enough to finish the bottle. Perhaps I’ll step it up and check in on a VV, as your note suggests there’s pleasure to be had.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Good to hear, I have some 06 Corbeaux!

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Curious when is the last time you tasted through a bunch of ‘06s? I’ve had a pretty good hit rate recently. I bet heavily on some of my favorite producers: Dujac, l’Arlot, Mugneret-Gibourg, Arnoux, Chevillon Cailles, Bachelet, even a couple of lesser Leroy.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Sadly I don’t have any 2006 Bachelet, but have very much enjoyed other producers this vintage and have been actively backfilling 2006 Burgs. Mugnier Clos de La Marechale, Dujac Combottes and Hudelot-Noellat Clos Vougeot have each been stand outs the last few years. Early maturity yes, and the vintage seems to lean towards earthy power rather than delicacy, but I’m ok with that. Given how these 1ers and a GC are now, I am not surprised to hear that village wines are also in a good place. I’ve been hunting for them but not much is showing up - I’d assumed that most had already been drunk.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not a vintage I reach for that often, I admit, and also a few years before I started buying wine in earnest, and not a vintage I have ever really backfilled… My 06 Bachelet is the fruit of an amicable exchange with the late Becky Wasserman during covid - a few thousand facemasks was the trade!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Not a vintage I reach for that often, I admit, and also a few years before I started buying wine in earnest, and not a vintage I have ever really backfilled… My 06 Bachelet is the fruit of an amicable exchange with the late Becky Wasserman during covid - a few thousand facemasks was the trade!

Great trade. I’ve had poor success with 06s for the most part, so good 06s are particular standouts for me. Fourrier and Mugneret-Gibourg both nailed the vintage, whereas I’ve really disliked every 06 Dujac I’ve had.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Love the trade story! Hate to hear that about Dujac. I have some. Don’t think I have drunk many. Not recently at least.

posts.name: Blair Curtis
posts.cooked:
2006 Duajc Combottes was killer at age 8.

Opened a 2006 Bachelet Charmes in late 2021 for a Paulee-style dinner and it was spectacular - one of the wines of the event.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Blair_Curtis:

2006 Duajc Combottes was killer at age 8.

Opened a 2006 Bachelet Charmes in late 2021 for a Paulee-style dinner and it was spectacular - one of the wines of the event.

Different strokes! I’ve had the Bonnes Mares, the Combottes and the Clos de la Roche and they’ve all been impenetrable. The Bonnes Mares (not my wine, but purchased by a friend on release) was starkly different to a hauntingly beautiful 96 Clos St. Denis at the same event.
